title: CodegenManifest
description: The outbound message across the code-generation seam (spec §5.2): the boundary between the deterministic oracle and a language backend. NOT reification — reify(AIO,context)→CIO (§4.5) is a different boundary; this seam turns the whole oracle into a target-language source tree. The manifest carries the WHOLE ORACLE BY VALUE — every fact a backend needs to emit code is inlined, resolved, and pre-computed, so a backend is a pure function of the manifest and calls nothing back. A backend is any process that consumes a manifest on stdin and answers a CodegenFilePlan on stdout. What crosses, by value: per-aggregate payload schemas (declared §3.2 fields taking PRECEDENCE over inference); Decider and Projector scenarios (the behavioural oracle, §6.3.1); flow chains with EVERY step's outcome pre-computed by the oracle (§3.4); screen facts — surfaces, offers, non-waived degraded states, and a present-state fixture already folded by the relevant Projector (§3.4); and the graph content hash the manifest was computed from (`graph_hash`). Payload values obey the wire scalar alphabet (§3.3): `boolean`, 64-bit signed `integer`, `string`; a field declared `number` or `date` (§3.2) travels AS A STRING. Field names are snake_case to match the codegen family. Versioned via `manifest_version` so a future shape change is a version bump, not a silent break. Self-contained: no cross-file `$ref`; every reusable shape inlines in `$defs`.

manifest_version.description: Codegen manifest wire version. Const "1" for this projection. A backend MUST reject a manifest whose version it does not understand rather than guess — the version gates the whole shape, so a bump is an explicit, non-silent break.

graph_hash.description: The content hash of the knowledge graph this manifest was computed from — `sha256:`-prefixed, lowercase hex. It IS the manifest's provenance anchor: a file plan generated from this manifest is attributable to exactly this graph state, and the consumer's drift gate (§7.3.1) compares against it. See also `$defs/graphHash`.

payload_schemas.description: Per-aggregate payload schemas, keyed by aggregate id. For each aggregate, the field shapes of its events, commands, and view — with DECLARED §3.2 fields taking precedence over anything the oracle could infer from scenarios. A backend types generated code from these, not from guesswork. Value type per `$defs/aggregateSchema`.

decider_scenarios.description: The Decider oracle (§6.3.1): each scenario folds `given` into fresh aggregate state and decides `when`, with the oracle's PRE-COMPUTED outcome in `then` — an accept-with-emit or a reject naming the invariant. A backend reproduces these; it does not re-derive them.

projector_scenarios.description: The Projector oracle (§6.3.1): each scenario folds `given` from the relevant Projector's initial view, with the oracle's PRE-COMPUTED resulting view in `then` (full-state). A backend reproduces these exactly.

flows.description: Flow chains (§3.4): an ordered command sequence where EVERY step's outcome is pre-computed by the oracle and inlined. The backend wires the flow; it never has to run the Decider to know what a step does.

screens.description: Screen facts (§3.4): per screen, the surfaces it shows, the offers (commands) it can invoke, its NON-WAIVED degraded states, and a present-state fixture already folded by the relevant Projector. Everything the backend needs to render a screen is by value here.

scalar.description: A wire scalar (§3.3): `boolean`, a 64-bit signed `integer`, or a `string`. A payload field declared `number` or `date` (§3.2) is carried here AS A STRING — the wire alphabet has no fractional-number or date type.

declaredType.description: A field's DECLARED type (§3.2), which takes precedence over inference. Note the split from the wire alphabet (§3.3): `boolean`, `integer`, and `string` cross as themselves, whereas a `number` or `date` field crosses the wire AS A STRING even though it is declared with its richer type here.

fieldSet.description: One message's or view's declared fields: field name → declared type (§3.2). Empty object means the empty payload.

aggregateSchema.description: One aggregate's payload schemas by value: the declared field shape of each of its events and commands, and of its view. Declared §3.2 fields win over inference.

events.description: Event id → its declared field set (§3.2).

commands.description: Command id → its declared field set (§3.2).

view.description: The aggregate's view: field name → declared type (§3.2).

eventRef.description: A reference to an event with its payload. EITHER a bare id string (that event with the EMPTY payload) OR an object naming the `event` id and its `with` payload. A missing `with` is the empty payload. Payload values are wire scalars (§3.3).

oneOf.description: Bare event id — the event with an empty payload.

with.description: The event payload: field name → wire scalar. Absent means the empty payload.

commandRef.description: A reference to a command with its payload. EITHER a bare id string (that command with the EMPTY payload) OR an object naming the `command` id and its `with` payload. A missing `with` is the empty payload. Payload values are wire scalars (§3.3).

oneOf.description: Bare command id — the command with an empty payload.

with.description: The command payload: field name → wire scalar. Absent means the empty payload.

decisionOutcome.description: The oracle's pre-computed result of deciding a command: an accept that emits events (`emit`) or a reject naming the invariant (`reject`). REJECT WINS if both are present — a step with both an emission and a rejection to report is a rejection.

emit.description: Events produced on accept, in emission order. May be empty (`[]`) — an accept with no event. Ignored if `reject` is present.

reject.description: The id of the invariant that rejected the command. Its presence makes the outcome a rejection regardless of any `emit`: reject wins.

deciderScenario.description: One Decider scenario with the oracle's pre-computed outcome (§6.3.1). Fold `given` into fresh state, decide `when`, expect `then`.

aggregate.description: Aggregate id this scenario exercises (keys into `payload_schemas`). Optional when unambiguous.

given.description: Prior events folded, in order, into fresh state. May be empty.

when.description: The single command decided against the folded state.

projectorScenario.description: One Projector scenario with the oracle's pre-computed view (§6.3.1). Fold `given` from the Projector's initial view; expect the full-state `then`.

projector.description: Projector id this scenario exercises. Optional when unambiguous.

given.description: Events folded, in order, from the initial view. May be empty.

then.description: The oracle's pre-computed view state (full-state): field name → wire scalar (§3.3).

flow.description: A flow chain (§3.4): an ordered command sequence whose every step outcome is pre-computed by the oracle.

steps.description: The flow's steps, in order. Each has a command and its oracle-pre-computed outcome.

when.description: The command issued at this step.

then.description: The oracle's pre-computed outcome of this step.

screen.description: A screen fact (§3.4): everything a backend needs to render one screen, by value.

surfaces.description: The view surfaces this screen shows (e.g. projector/view ids it reads from).

offers.description: The offers this screen can invoke — commands the user may issue from it. Each is a CommandRef (bare id, or id with a partially-bound `with`).

degraded_states.description: The screen's NON-WAIVED degraded states — the ones a realiser must still handle (waived states are already dropped by the oracle and never appear here).

description.description: What this degraded state means / when it shows.

present_state.description: A present-state fixture: the screen's view already folded by the relevant Projector, field name → wire scalar (§3.3). Lets the backend scaffold against a concrete, oracle-computed state.

graphHash.description: A `sha256:`-prefixed, lowercase-hex content hash of the knowledge graph a manifest was computed from. Ties every generated artifact back to an immutable graph state so the consumer's provenance/drift gate (§7.3.1) is reproducible.
